title: How to Evaluate a Designer's Portfolio
excerpt: Most people look at a portfolio and simply ask "do I like this?" But that's not enough to make a confident hiring decision. This post gives you a systematic framework to evaluate a web designer's portfolio for quality, strategic depth, and relevance to your project.
content:
When hiring a web or app designer, the portfolio is your most important data point. But knowing how to evaluate it is a skill in itself. Here's a framework for assessing a designer's portfolio effectively.

Step 1: Look for Work That's Similar to Your Project

A designer who has built e-commerce sites for fashion brands may not be the best fit for a B2B software company. Look for work that's similar in:

  • Industry (do they understand your sector?)

  • Type (brochure site vs. web app vs. e-commerce vs. mobile app)

  • Scale (a portfolio full of one-page sites may indicate limited experience with complex projects)


Step 2: Visit the Live Sites

Many portfolios show polished screenshots. Always visit the live URLs. Check:

  • Is the site still live and maintained?

  • Does it load quickly?

  • Does it look as good in the browser as in the screenshot?

  • Is the mobile version well-executed?


Step 3: Look for Strategic Thinking, Not Just Visual Craft

A great portfolio includes context. Does the designer explain why they made certain decisions? Do their case studies describe business goals, challenges, and measurable outcomes? Visual beauty is important, but strategic thinking is what drives results.

Step 4: Assess Consistency

One standout project in a sea of mediocre work is a red flag. Look for consistent quality across multiple projects. Every piece in the portfolio should meet your minimum quality bar.

Step 5: Evaluate the Range of Complexity

Can the designer handle both simple and complex projects? A portfolio that only shows simple sites may indicate limited capability for more ambitious work.

Step 6: Notice the Details

The best designers sweat the details — precise spacing, perfect alignment, thoughtful micro-interactions, meticulous typography. Imprecise work in a portfolio will be even more imprecise under the pressure of a real project.
